354 
Supreme Court Reports
intended to come into effect only after the death of the 
testator and is revocable at any time during the lifetime of 
the testator. It is said that so long as the testator is alive, 
a will is not worth the paper on which it is written, as the 
testator can at any time revoke it. If the testator, who is 
not married, marries after making the will, by operation 
of law, the will stands revoked. Registration of a will does 
not make it any more effective.”
25. This Court on the issue of the proof of Wills in the case of 
H. Venkatachala Iyengar v. B.N. Thimmajamma and Others8 has 
succinctly defined the contours as under:
“18. What is the true legal position in the matter of proof 
of wills? It is well-known that the proof of wills presents 
a recurring topic for decision in courts and there are a 
large number of judicial pronouncements on the subject. 
The party propounding a will or otherwise making a claim
